mappings.rationale: Directly addresses the nature and scope of literature, literary genres, critical approaches, and theoretical treatises about literature — core topics of this section.
Covers the history and forms of English literature, directly relevant to the section's treatment of literature of Western peoples.
Covers a major Western literary tradition, relevant to the section's treatment of literature of Western peoples.
Covers a major Western literary tradition, relevant to the section's treatment of literature of Western peoples.
Covers a major Western literary tradition, relevant to the section's treatment of literature of Western peoples.
Covers a major Western literary tradition, relevant to the section's treatment of literature of Western peoples.
Covers a major Western literary tradition, relevant to the section's treatment of literature of Western peoples.
Covers a major non-Western literary tradition, extending the section's scope beyond Western peoples.
Covers a major non-Western literary tradition, extending the section's scope beyond Western peoples.
Covers Latin American literary traditions blending indigenous and European forms, relevant to the section's broad scope of literary history.
Covers modern Latin American literary forms and movements, relevant to the section's treatment of narrative fiction and poetry.
Covers a distinctive literary tradition relevant to the section's treatment of primitive, folk, and popular literature.
Covers a major literary tradition with roots in religious and doctrinal writing, relevant to multiple categories in the section.
Covers ancient Greek and Roman literature including epic, tragedy, comedy, and lyric poetry — genres explicitly listed in this section.
Covers medieval literary forms including romance, saga, and religious drama — genres explicitly listed in this section.
Addresses the comparative study of literary works across languages and traditions, relevant to the section's coverage of literary genres and scholarship.
Directly covers tragedy as a dramatic-theatrical literary form, explicitly listed as a kind of literary composition in section C.3.a.
Directly covers comedy as a dramatic-theatrical literary form, explicitly listed as a kind of literary composition in section C.3.b.
Covers Gothic romance and Gothic literary traditions, relevant to the section's treatment of romance and narrative imaginative literature.
Covers science fiction as a literary genre, relevant to the section's treatment of kinds of narrative imaginative literature.
Covers crime fiction as a literary genre, relevant to the section's treatment of the novel, the short story, and popular literature.
Covers horror as a literary genre spanning fiction, drama, and poetry, relevant to the section's treatment of narrative imaginative literature.
Directly relevant to the section's coverage of fable, parable, allegory, and related forms, as well as folk and popular literature.
Directly covers the short story and its antecedents, explicitly listed as a kind of literary composition in section C.1.e.
Directly covers epic as a literary form including literary epic, beast epic, mock epic, and romantic epic — explicitly listed in section C.2.a.
Directly covers poetry as a kind of literary composition, explicitly listed in section C.1, including distinctions between verse and prose.
Covers American poetic traditions, relevant to the section's treatment of poetry and lyric literature.
Covers contemporary novels and fiction, relevant to the section's treatment of the novel and current literary forms.
Relevant to the section's treatment of popular literature and literature's relationship with its audience.
Directly covers children's literature, explicitly listed as a kind of literary composition in section C.7.
Directly covers rhetoric as the art of discourse, including figures of speech and prose style — explicitly treated in section B.1.
Directly covers autobiographical literature, explicitly listed under nonfictional prose in section C.6.o.
Directly covers biographical literature including critical and interpretive biography, explicitly listed under nonfictional prose in section C.6.o.
Explores the relationship between literature and its audience, a topic explicitly covered in section A.4.
Relevant to the section's treatment of the distinction between literature and other forms of writing, and the nature of literary composition.
Relevant to the section's treatment of literary diction and the tools of literary composition.
Covers Romantic literature including poetry, novel, and drama — major genres in Western literary history discussed in section D.
Covers literary modernism as a major movement in Western literature, relevant to section D on the history of literature.
Covers the Decadent literary movement, relevant to the section's treatment of Western literary history.
Covers the Beat literary movement in American literature, relevant to Western literary history.
Covers Bohemian literary culture and its influence on literary movements, relevant to Western literary history.
Shakespeare is central to dramatic literature including tragedy, comedy, and lyric poetry — all explicitly listed in this section.
Directly covers Shakespearean comedy, relevant to the section's treatment of comedy as dramatic literature.
Directly covers Shakespearean tragedy, relevant to the section's treatment of tragedy as dramatic literature.
Covers Shakespeare's lyric poetry including sonnets, directly relevant to section C.4.b on language-based lyrics.
Chaucer's works span narrative poetry, romance, and fable — genres explicitly listed in this section's outline.
Dante's works span epic, allegory, and lyric poetry — genres explicitly listed in this section.
Homer's works are foundational epics, directly relevant to the section's treatment of epic as narrative imaginative literature.
Ovid's works span epic, lyric, and narrative poetry, relevant to multiple literary genres listed in this section.
Horace's odes, satires, and epistles are foundational examples of lyric, satiric, and epistolary literature — genres explicitly listed in this section.
Sophocles is a foundational tragedian, directly relevant to the section's treatment of tragedy as dramatic literature.
Goethe's works span drama, lyric poetry, and the novel — major literary forms covered in this section.
Austen is a major novelist, directly relevant to the section's treatment of the novel as narrative imaginative literature.
Dickens is a major novelist and social satirist, relevant to the section's treatment of the novel and satiric literature.
Tolstoy is a major novelist, relevant to the section's treatment of the novel and nonfictional prose.
Dostoevsky is a major novelist and philosophical writer, relevant to the section's treatment of the novel and philosophical literature.
Joyce is a major modernist novelist, relevant to the section's treatment of the novel and Western literary history.
Proust is a major modernist novelist, relevant to the section's treatment of the novel and Western literary history.
James is a major novelist and literary critic, relevant to the section's treatment of the novel and criticism as literature.
Eliot is a major novelist, relevant to the section's treatment of the novel as narrative imaginative literature.
Trollope is a major Victorian novelist, relevant to the section's treatment of the novel as narrative imaginative literature.
Shelley's Frankenstein spans Gothic romance and science fiction, relevant to the section's treatment of romance and narrative fiction.
Christie is a major crime fiction author, relevant to the section's treatment of popular literature and the novel.
Tolkien's works are foundational fantasy literature drawing on epic and romance traditions explicitly listed in this section.
Lewis wrote fantasy, children's literature, and critical-theological prose — forms explicitly listed in this section.
Kafka is a major modernist fiction writer, relevant to the section's treatment of the novel, the short story, and parable.
Camus wrote novels, plays, and essays — multiple literary forms covered in this section — and is a major figure in Western literary history.
Zola is a major naturalist novelist, relevant to the section's treatment of the novel and Western literary history.
Shaw is a major dramatist and essayist, relevant to the section's treatment of dramatic literature and the essay.
Bishop is a major American poet, relevant to the section's treatment of lyric poetry.
Plath is a major American poet and prose writer, relevant to the section's treatment of lyric poetry and autobiographical literature.
Thoreau's works span the essay, travel literature, and philosophical literature — nonfictional prose forms explicitly listed in this section.
Montaigne is the founder of the essay as a literary form, directly relevant to section C.6.a on the essay.
Voltaire wrote satire, philosophical tales, and polemical literature — forms explicitly listed in this section.
Machiavelli's works are classics of political literature, a form of nonfictional prose explicitly listed in section C.6.f.
Sade's literary works span the novel, philosophical literature, and dramatic literature — forms covered in this section.
Barthes contributed major works of literary criticism and theory, relevant to section A.7 on writings about literature.
Directly addresses doctrinal and religious texts as literature, relevant to section C.6.d on doctrinal and religious literature.
Directly addresses religious texts as literature, relevant to section C.6.d on doctrinal and religious literature.
Relevant to the section's treatment of rhetoric, grammar, syntax, and the manipulation of language in literary composition.
Relevant to the section's treatment of literary diction, style, and the relationship between rhetoric and grammar.
Relevant to the section's treatment of the relationship between language, audience, and literary style.
Relevant to literary composition across languages and the relationship between literature and its audience in different linguistic contexts.

mappings.vsiAuthor: Emma Borg and Sarah A. Fisher
mappings.rationale: Relevant to the section's treatment of meaning in literary texts and the interpretation of literary works.
Relevant to the section's treatment of interpretation of literary works and critical methods.
A major approach to literary criticism and interpretation, relevant to section A.7 on writings about literature.
Postcolonial literary criticism is a major critical approach, relevant to section A.7 on writings about literature and to non-Western literary traditions.
Feminist literary criticism is a major critical approach relevant to the section's treatment of writings about literature.
Relevant to the section's treatment of comedy, farce, satire, and parody as literary forms.
Relevant to the section's treatment of literary diction, style, and the relationship between rhetoric and popular language.
Explores the role of imagination in literary composition and the creative process of writing, relevant to sections A.2 and A.3.
Myth is a foundational source for literary forms including epic, saga, and fable — genres explicitly listed in this section.
Derrida's literary deconstruction is a major critical approach, relevant to section A.7 on writings about literature.
Directly covers modern dramatic literature, relevant to section C.3 on dramatic or theatrical literature.
Relevant to the section's treatment of prosody, language processing, and the relationship between language and literary comprehension.
Covers Melville's novels and poetry including Moby-Dick, exploring themes of inequality, democracy, philosophy, and religion in nineteenth-century American literature.
Explores Borges's contributions to postmodern literature, his use of labyrinths and the infinite, and his place in Argentine and world literature.
